translator: Literal-ish public-domain rendering by Robot Mason
1.1: Now, the teaching of Yoga begins.
1.2: Yoga is the cessation of the fluctuations of consciousness.
1.3: Then the Seer rests in its own true nature.
1.4: At other times, the Seer is identified with the fluctuations.
1.5: The fluctuations are five; they are painful or not.
1.6: They are right knowledge, wrong knowledge, imagination, sleep, and memory.
1.7: Right knowledge arises from direct perception, inference, and reliable testimony.
1.8: Wrong knowledge is false understanding not based on the thing itself.
1.9: Imagination follows words and concepts devoid of substance.
1.10: Sleep is a fluctuation supported by the experience of absence.
1.11: Memory is the retention of experienced objects.
1.12: Their cessation is by practice and dispassion.
1.13: Practice is the effort to stabilize the mind there.
1.14: It is firmly grounded when done for a long time, without break, with devotion.
1.15: Dispassion is mastery over craving for seen and heard-of objects.
1.16: Higher dispassion is indifference to the gunas through knowledge of the Self.
1.17: Samprajnata samadhi is accompanied by reasoning, reflection, bliss, and I-ness.
1.18: Asamprajnata samadhi follows when only latent impressions remain after cessation.
1.19: Some attain through birth or dissolution into nature; their samadhi is innate.
1.20: For others it is preceded by faith, vigor, memory, samadhi, and insight.
1.21: For the intensely resolute, attainment is near.
1.22: Even among them there is a distinction: mild, medium, or intense.
1.24: Ishvara is a special Purusha, untouched by afflictions, actions, fruits, and latent seeds.
1.25: In Him the seed of omniscience is unsurpassed.
1.26: Unconditioned by time, He is teacher of even the ancients.
1.27: His designation is the syllable Om.
1.28: Japa of Om with contemplation of its meaning.
1.29: From that, inward consciousness is attained and obstacles fall away.
1.30: Disease, apathy, doubt, carelessness, laziness, sense-indulgence, false views, non-attainment, and instability—these distract the mind.
1.31: Pain, depression, bodily tremor, and irregular breathing accompany distraction.
1.32: To remove them, practice one truth-principle.
1.33: Cultivate friendliness to the happy, compassion for the suffering, joy in the virtuous, and equanimity toward the non-virtuous—thus the mind becomes clear.
1.34: Or by exhalation and retention of the breath.
1.35: Or by meditating on a sense-object that steadies the mind.
1.36: Or on a sorrowless inner light.
1.37: Or on a mind free from attachment.
1.38: Or on knowledge derived from dreams or sleep.
1.39: Or by meditation on anything one chooses as elevating.
1.40: Mastery extends from the smallest atom to the greatest.
1.41: When fluctuations are thinned, the mind is like a clear jewel, taking the color of the knower, the knowing, or the known—this is samapatti.
1.42: Vitarka-samapatti: cognitive absorption with verbalization, meaning, and knowledge, mixed with conceptualization.
1.43: When memory is purified, the mind is emptied of its own nature; as though transparent, it rests on the object alone—this is nirvitarka.
1.44: Similarly with subtler objects: savichara and nirvichara absorptions.
1.45: The most subtle object is prakriti’s unmanifest.
1.46: These absorptions with seed are samprajnata.
1.47: In nirvichara clarity, pure insight dawns.
1.49: It differs from inference and testimony, for it concerns particulars.
1.50: Its latent impression counteracts other impressions.
1.51: When even that impression is restrained, seedless samadhi arises.
2.1: Tapas, svadhyaya, and devotion to Ishvara constitute Kriya Yoga.
2.2: Its aim is to attenuate afflictions and bring samadhi.
2.3: The afflictions are ignorance, egoism, attachment, aversion, and clinging to life.
2.4: Ignorance is the field for the others, whether dormant, weakened, interrupted, or active.
2.5: Ignorance is seeing the impermanent as permanent, impure as pure, painful as pleasant, non-Self as Self.
2.6: Egoism is identification of the Seer with the instruments of seeing.
2.9: Clinging to life arises even in the wise and flows on by its own force.
2.10: In their subtle form they are to be resolved back into their source.
2.11: In their gross form they are eliminated by meditation.
2.12: Past karmas have root in afflictions and bear fruits seen in life-span, circumstances, and tendencies.
2.13: While the root exists, they fructify as birth, life, and experience.
2.14: Because of the changing of gunas and conflicting tendencies, life’s experiences are painful.
2.15: Thus, for the discerning, everything is suffering—due to change, anguish, impressions, and the gunas’ activities.
2.16: Suffering not yet arisen is to be avoided.
2.17: The conjunction of Seer and seen is the cause of suffering.
2.18: The seen is of the nature of the gunas—illumination, activity, inertia—forming elements, organs, mind; it exists for experience and liberation.
2.19: The stages of the gunas are specific, non-specific, mere sign, and unmanifest.
2.20: The Seer is pure seeing, though appearing to see through the mind.
2.21: The seen exists only for the Seer.
2.22: Though it ceases for one who has attained, it continues to exist for others as common ground.
2.23: Their conjunction is for the Seer’s recognition of its true powers.
2.25: With the disappearance of ignorance, the conjunction ends—this is Kaivalya, the Seer standing alone.
2.26: Uninterrupted discriminative knowledge is the means to its end.
2.27: Sevenfold is the highest insight’s progression.
2.28: By practicing the limbs of Yoga, impurities are destroyed; the light of knowledge leads to discriminative insight.
2.29: The eight limbs are restraints, observances, posture, breath regulation, sense withdrawal, concentration, meditation, and samadhi.
2.30: The restraints are non-violence, truthfulness, non-stealing, continence, and non-greed.
2.31: These are the great vow—universal, unrestricted by birth, place, time, or duty.
2.32: The observances are purity, contentment, austerity, self-study, and devotion to Ishvara.
2.33: When afflicted by negative thoughts, cultivate their opposites.
2.34: Negative thoughts—violence, etc.—whether done, caused, or approved, arise from greed, anger, or delusion, mild to intense; they bring endless suffering and ignorance. Oppose them with their contrary.
2.35: In the presence of established non-violence, enmity ceases.
2.36: When truthfulness is established, actions bear fruits in accord.
2.37: When non-stealing is established, all jewels appear.
2.38: When continence is established, vigor is gained.
2.39: From non-greed arises knowledge of the how and why of past and future births.
2.40: From purity comes disgust for one’s own body and separation from others’ bodies.
2.41: From purity also come cheerfulness, one-pointedness, mastery of the senses, and fitness for Self-realization.
2.43: From austerity, impurities are burned and powers of body and senses are perfected.
2.44: From self-study, communion with the desired deity.
2.47: It is mastered by relaxing effort and meditating on the infinite.
2.49: After posture, pranayama is the pause of inhale and exhale.
2.50: It is external, internal, or stopped; measured by place, time, and number; long and subtle.
2.51: The fourth transcends the spheres of inhale and exhale.
2.52: From that the covering of light is thinned.
2.53: And the mind becomes fit for concentration.
2.54: Sense withdrawal is the senses’ imitation of the mind by disengaging from their objects.
2.55: Then comes supreme mastery over the senses.
3.1: Concentration is fixing the mind on one place.
3.2: Meditation is continuous flow of cognition toward that place.
3.3: Samadhi is the shining of the object alone, as if empty of self.
3.5: By mastering samyama comes light of knowledge.
3.7: These three are inner compared to the previous two limbs.
3.8: But even they are external to seedless samadhi.
3.9: By restraint of the rising tendencies and emergence of restraint-impressions, transformation toward samadhi occurs.
3.10: With the subsidence of fluctuations, the mind reaches tranquility.
3.11: In samadhi-transformation, distraction diminishes and one-pointedness arises.
3.12: In one-pointedness-transformation, the same thought appears and reappears.
3.13: By these are explained transformations of characteristics, state, and condition.
3.14: Underlying is a substrate whose transformations produce different states.
3.15: Cause and effect relation explains sequence of transformations.
3.16: By samyama on the threefold transformations, knowledge of past and future.
3.17: By samyama on sound—its meaning and idea—knowledge of another’s thought.
3.18: By direct perception of latent impressions, knowledge of previous births.
3.19: By cognition of mental images, knowledge of another’s mind.
3.20: But not of its supporting factors, for that is not the object of samyama.
3.21: By samyama on the body’s form, suspending its perceptibility, the yogin becomes invisible.
3.22: Karma is fast or slow; by samyama on it, knowledge of death.
3.23: By samyama on friendliness, compassion, and joy, those qualities are acquired.
3.24: By samyama on strength of elephants and the like, strength is obtained.
3.25: By samyama on the inner light, knowledge of the subtle, hidden, and distant.
3.26: By samyama on the sun, knowledge of the worlds.
3.27: By samyama on the moon, knowledge of the arrangement of stars.
3.28: By samyama on the pole-star, knowledge of their motions.
3.29: By samyama on the navel center, knowledge of the constitution of the body.
3.30: By samyama on the throat, cessation of hunger and thirst.
3.31: By samyama on the tortoise-channel, steadiness.
3.32: By samyama on the light in the head, vision of perfected beings.
3.33: Or knowledge of everything from intuition.
3.34: By samyama on the heart, knowledge of the mind.
3.35: Experience arises from failing to distinguish between Purusha and sattva; by samyama on the Self, knowledge of Purusha and independence.
3.36: Thence, superhuman hearing, touch, sight, taste, and smell.
3.37: They are obstacles to samadhi, powers in worldly states.
3.38: When the cause of attachment to body is loosened, the mind can enter another’s body.
3.39: By mastering the upward life-breath, one can rise above water, mud, thorns, and so on.
3.40: By samyama on the relation of ear and ether, divine hearing.
3.41: By samyama on the relation of body and ether, lightness of cotton and passage through space.
3.42: By samyama on the thought-processes, knowledge of previous births.
3.43: By samyama on the relation of the elements—their gross, subtle, inherent, and purposive forms—mastery over elements.
3.44: From that, minuteness, greatness, lightness, heaviness, reach, and command over them.
3.45: Perfection of the body: beauty, grace, strength, and adamantine firmness.
3.46: By samyama on the senses—their form and mastery—control over them and rapidity of mind.
3.47: From that, mastery over the primordial; independence of the senses and mind.
3.48: For one established in Self, mastery over all states.
3.49: By detachment from even that, the seed of bondage is destroyed; omniscience and omnipotence awaken.
3.50: By dispassion, when addressed by lords of creation, no attachment or pride.
3.51: By intuitive discrimination between sattva and Purusha, sovereignty over all and omniscience; and freedom.
3.52: When invited by subtle beings, avoid attachment, or knowledge and elevation may be lost.
3.53: By samyama on moments and their sequence, discrimination-knowledge.
3.54: From that arises knowledge distinguishing similar objects with subtle differences.
3.55: Supreme discriminative knowledge is instantaneous, all-comprehensive, and successive.
3.56: When purity of sattva equals Purusha, there is Kaivalya.
4.1: Perfections arise from birth, herbs, mantra, austerity, or samadhi.
4.2: Transformation into another species occurs by filling nature’s causes.
4.3: Incidental causes aid but do not produce the transformation; like a farmer opening a channel for water.
4.4: Minds are many, arising from differentiated egoism.
4.5: One mind directs the many activities, distinguished by their latent impressions.
4.6: Of those born of meditation, there is no sowing of karmic seeds.
4.7: Actions of the yogin are neither white nor black; others’ are threefold.
4.8: From them, only those impressions arise that fit their causes.
4.9: Because of sameness of memory and impressions, the flow continues even across births.
4.10: Impressions have no beginning; the impulse to life is eternal.
4.11: They are held together by cause, effect, substratum, and support; when these cease, impressions cease.
4.12: Past and future exist in their own forms; differences of sequence are in the properties.
4.13: The properties manifest as specific, non-specific, and balanced.
4.14: The essence of a thing is an underlying field of properties.
4.15: Though the object is the same, it is seen differently according to the mind.
4.16: An object does not depend on a single mind; otherwise its destruction would follow that mind’s cessation.
4.17: The object is known or unknown depending on whether the mind is colored by it.
4.18: The mind is perceived by the unchanging Seer.
4.19: The mind is not self-luminous, for it is an object.
4.20: One mind cannot perceive both itself and the object simultaneously.
4.21: If one mind were perceived by another, infinite regress would ensue.
4.22: When the mind takes the form of the Seer and the seen, it understands itself.
4.23: The mind, colored by seer and seen, is all-knowing.
4.24: Though variegated by countless tendencies, the mind exists for another—the Purusha, therefore it is for-the-sake-of.
4.25: For one who has seen the distinction, the mind ceases to act in conjunction.
4.26: Then the mind tends toward discrimination and moves toward dissolution.
4.27: Even then, rising impressions from past tendencies arise; because of them there is obstruction by residual afflictions.
4.28: They are removed as were the afflictions.
4.29: For one uninterested even in omniscience, established in discrimination, there is seedless samadhi.
4.30: From dispassion even toward that, the residual afflictions end.
4.31: Then, with the covering removed, knowledge is infinite, little remains to be known.
4.32: Then the sequence of transformations of the gunas comes to an end, having fulfilled their purpose.
4.33: Sequence is the moment recognized by the end of preceding and beginning of following.
4.34: When the gunas, devoid of purpose for the Purusha, return to their source, Kaivalya shines: the Seer abides in its own nature.
